one_liner: A 28-year-old woman with a history of recurrent UTIs presents with fever, right flank pain, and dysuria for 2 days.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient reports 3 days of urinary frequency, urgency, and burning with urination, followed by the onset of right-sided flank pain, fevers up to 39.4°C, chills, and rigors over the past 2 days. She has had nausea with one episode of vomiting. She has had 4 UTIs in the past 2 years, all treated with oral antibiotics. She is sexually active with one male partner and uses oral contraceptive pills. She denies vaginal discharge, hematuria, or recent urinary instrumentation.
stage2.pmh: Recurrent urinary tract infections, irritable bowel syndrome.
stage2.family_history: Mother with recurrent UTIs. No family history of kidney disease.
stage2.social_history: Graduate student. Non-smoker, occasional alcohol use. Sexually active with one partner. No illicit drug use.
stage2.physical_exam: Young woman appearing flushed and uncomfortable. Mild diaphoresis. Heart: tachycardic, regular rhythm. Lungs: clear bilaterally. Abdomen: soft, non-distended, mild suprapubic tenderness, no rebound or guarding; Marked right costovertebral angle tenderness on percussion; No left CVA tenderness; No vaginal discharge on external inspection. Extremities: warm, no edema.

lab_results.result: Cloudy, pH 6.5, large leukocyte esterase, positive nitrites, >100 WBCs/hpf, moderate bacteria, 5-10 RBCs/hpf, WBC casts present
lab_results.clinical_significance: Pyuria with WBC casts localizes infection to the kidney (this condition); positive nitrites suggest gram-negative bacteria

lab_results.result: Escherichia coli >100,000 CFU/mL; sensitive to ciprofloxacin, ceftriaxone, gentamicin; resistant to ampicillin and trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole
lab_results.clinical_significance: Confirms E. coli UTI with antibiotic sensitivities; guides definitive therapy
Leukocytosis with neutrophilic predominance and left shift indicates bacterial infection
Normal renal function; uncomplicated this condition typically preserves GFR
No bacteremia; supports uncomplicated this condition
Markedly elevated inflammatory marker consistent with upper urinary tract infection
Normal; no evidence of sepsis-related tissue hypoperfusion
Elevated procalcitonin helps distinguish upper tract infection (this condition) from lower tract infection (cystitis)

imaging_results.result: Right kidney mildly enlarged at 13.2 cm with mild perinephric stranding. No hydronephrosis, abscess, or calculi identified. Left kidney normal.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Renal enlargement and perinephric stranding consistent with the suspected diagnosis; no obstructing stone or abscess

imaging_results.result: Not performed (reserved for complicated cases or failure to improve on antibiotics).
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Not indicated in uncomplicated this condition

stage5.final_clue: Fever, right CVA tenderness, pyuria with WBC casts (localizing infection to the kidney), positive nitrites, and E. coli >100,000 CFU/mL on urine culture.

debrief.classic_presentation: A young woman with recurrent UTIs presents with high fever, chills, flank pain, CVA tenderness, and lower urinary tract symptoms (dysuria, frequency, urgency). UA shows pyuria with WBC casts, and culture grows >100,000 CFU/mL of E. coli.
debrief.pathophysiology: Acute pyelonephritis is a bacterial infection of the renal parenchyma and collecting system, most commonly caused by ascending infection from the lower urinary tract. E. coli accounts for 80-90% of cases. Virulence factors such as P fimbriae facilitate bacterial adhesion to uroepithelium and ascent to the kidney. Risk factors include female anatomy (short urethra), sexual activity, prior UTIs, urinary obstruction, and vesicoureteral reflux. The infection triggers an intense inflammatory response with neutrophilic infiltration of the renal interstitium. WBC casts in urine are pathognomonic for renal parenchymal inflammation.
debrief.treatment_overview: Outpatient management for uncomplicated pyelonephritis: oral fluoroquinolone (ciprofloxacin 500 mg BID for 7 days) or TMP-SMX (if susceptible) for 14 days. Hospitalization criteria: inability to tolerate oral intake, sepsis, hemodynamic instability, pregnancy, or concern for complicated infection. Inpatient therapy: IV ceftriaxone 1g daily or IV fluoroquinolone. Switch to oral therapy once afebrile for 24-48 hours and clinically improving. Follow-up urine culture is generally not needed if symptoms resolve.
high_yield_pearls: WBC casts on urinalysis are pathognomonic for pyelonephritis and localize infection to the kidney parenchyma.
E. coli causes 80-90% of community-acquired pyelonephritis; other organisms include Klebsiella, Proteus, and Enterococcus.
Procalcitonin >0.5 ng/mL helps distinguish pyelonephritis from lower UTI (cystitis) and may guide treatment intensity.

Imaging is not routinely needed for uncomplicated pyelonephritis; CT is indicated if no improvement after 48-72 hours of appropriate antibiotics (to evaluate for abscess, obstruction, or emphysematous pyelonephritis).
Complicated pyelonephritis risk factors: male sex, pregnancy, diabetes, immunocompromised state, urinary tract abnormalities, indwelling catheter, or hospital-acquired infection.
sources: IDSA Guidelines: Diagnosis, Prevention, and Treatment of Catheter-Associated Urinary Tract Infection (2010) — https://www.idsociety.org/practice-guideline/?q=IDSA+Guidelines%3A+Diagnosis%2C+Prevention%2C+and+Treatment+of+Catheter-Associated+Urinary+Tract+Infection+%282010%29

Gupta K et al. International clinical practice guidelines for the treatment of acute uncomplicated cystitis and pyelonephritis in women. Clin Infect Dis. 2011;52(5):e103-e120. — https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=Gupta+K+et+al.+International+clinical+practice+guidelines+for+the+treatment+of+acute+uncomplicated+cystitis+and+pyelonephritis+in+women.+Clin+Infect+Dis.+2011%3B52%285%29%3Ae103-e120.
UpToDate: Acute uncomplicated pyelonephritis in women (2024) — https://www.uptodate.com/contents/search?search=Acute+uncomplicated+pyelonephritis+in+women+%282024%29

after_stage2_more_likely: Acute pyelonephritis — fever, chills, unilateral CVA tenderness, lower urinary tract symptoms, history of recurrent UTIs
Nephrolithiasis with infection — flank pain could be from a stone, but fever and dysuria suggest infectious process
after_stage2_less_likely: Acute appendicitis — pain is in the flank (not RLQ), and urinary symptoms predominate
Pelvic inflammatory disease — no vaginal discharge, pain is in the flank
after_labs_top3: Acute pyelonephritis — WBC casts, pyuria, E. coli >100K CFU/mL, elevated procalcitonin, no obstruction on imaging
Complicated UTI — heavy pyuria and bacteriuria, but WBC casts and elevated procalcitonin confirm upper tract involvement
Renal abscess — ultrasound shows no abscess; perinephric stranding is consistent with pyelonephritis
